name: Half-Wave Dipole
description: A fundamental wire antenna consisting of a straight conductor with length equal to half a wavelength at the operating frequency. It is center-fed and exhibits omnidirectional radiation in the plane perpendicular to its axis with a characteristic figure-8 pattern. The half-wave dipole serves as the reference antenna for gain measurements and is widely used in communications systems.

length.name: Total Length
length.description: Physical length of the dipole conductor

feedGap.name: Feed Gap
feedGap.description: Gap width at the center feed point

designMethodology.overview: Design process involves calculating the physical length for resonance, determining wire dimensions for practical construction, and optimizing the feed structure for impedance matching.

steps.title: Calculate Resonant Length
steps.description: Determine the physical length for half-wavelength resonance including end effects

steps.title: Select Wire Radius
steps.description: Choose conductor radius based on bandwidth and mechanical requirements
steps.formula: a = L / (2000 to 500) for typical applications

steps.title: Calculate Input Impedance
steps.description: Determine theoretical input impedance at resonance

steps.title: Design Feed Structure
steps.description: Design the feed gap and balun if required for balanced operation

steps.title: Optimize for Bandwidth
steps.description: Adjust length and radius ratio for desired bandwidth

steps.title: Verify Radiation Pattern
steps.description: Confirm omnidirectional pattern in H-plane and figure-8 in E-plane

equations.resonantFrequency: f = c / (2 * L_eff) where L_eff = 0.47 * λ
equations.inputImpedance: Zin = 73.1 + j * 42.5 * ln(L/a) ohms at resonance
equations.radiationPattern: E(θ) = cos(π/2 * cos(θ)) / sin(θ) in elevation plane

mockSolverHints.impedanceModel: Method of moments with sinusoidal current distribution I(z) = I0 * sin(π/2 - π*|z|/L)
mockSolverHints.radiationModel: Far-field integration using cos(π/2*cos(θ))/sin(θ) pattern function

references: Balanis, C.A., 'Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design', 4th Edition, Wiley, 2016, Chapter 4
Stutzman, W.L. and Thiele, G.A., 'Antenna Theory and Design', 3rd Edition, Wiley, 2012, Chapter 4
Kraus, J.D. and Marhefka, R.J., 'Antennas for All Applications', 3rd Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2002, Chapter 8
Volakis, J.L., 'Antenna Engineering Handbook', 4th Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2007, Chapter 5
